narration.default: Forget to water your plant for a week and the leaves go sad and droopy. What if the plant could just take care of itself? Today you'll build exactly that — a soil sensor that watches the dirt, and a pump that turns on the moment the soil goes dry. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you are in Upload mode. If you have not done that yet, finish the Blink an LED tutorial first.
Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. Setup runs once at the start. Loop runs forever — checking the soil over and over, all day long.

Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and leave mode on output — that's the pump. The soil sensor lives on pin A0 — analog inputs like A0 configure themselves automatically, so they don't need a pinMode line. Only outputs do.

steps.title: Wait — didn't we just do this?

narration.default: Hold on a second. A sensor on A0, an output on pin 9, an if-else picking between them based on a number… didn't we just do this with the night lamp? It really does feel the same. Let's stop and ask the question out loud — is this the same tutorial wearing a costume?

Yes — exactly the same shape. Sensor reads a number. The program compares the number to a threshold. Based on the answer, an output turns on or off. Light becomes moisture. Lamp becomes pump. The pattern transfers. Once you've seen this shape once, you'll start spotting it everywhere — in thermostats, alarms, level monitors, automatic taps. Same three steps, different parts plugged in.

steps.title: A box for the moisture reading

narration.default: Open Variables and click Make a Variable. Name it moisture. This will hold the latest reading from the soil sensor — a number we'll check every loop to decide whether the plant is thirsty.

Drag a set moisture block into the loop. For the value, drag an analogRead block from the Arduino category and drop it in place of the 0. Set analogRead's pin to A0. Same block you used for the night lamp's light sensor — analogRead gives back a number from 0 to 1023, no matter what kind of analog sensor is on the pin.

For the soil sensor, a lower number means drier dirt. A higher number means damp dirt. We pick a threshold — a line in the sand. Below this number the soil is too dry and the plant needs water. Let's pick 400 to start. You can tune it later, just like the night lamp's brightness number.

Drag an if-else block below the set moisture block. The if branch will handle the dry case — pump on. The else branch will handle the damp case — pump off. Same if-else block you've used in Scratch and in the night lamp.

Open Operators. Drag a less-than block into the diamond slot of the if-else. Drop the moisture variable on the left side. Change the right side to 400. The condition reads: 'if the moisture reading is below 400, the soil is dry — turn the pump on'.
Drag a digitalWrite block into the if branch. Set pin to 9 and keep value on high. When the soil reads below 400 — too dry — the pump kicks in.
Drag another digitalWrite into the else branch. Keep pin on 9 but change value to low — the pump shuts off when the soil is damp enough on its own. No flooding the plant.
Drag a delay block below the if-else. Set it to 500 milliseconds. The Arduino now checks the soil twice every second — plenty fast for a plant, gentle on the loop.

Open the Simulator and click the plus button. The simulator doesn't have a real soil-moisture probe — so we'll borrow the photoresistor and use it as a stand-in. Add a Photoresistor on pin A0. Pretend its slider is the soil dampness — slider down means dry dirt, slider up means damp dirt.

Click the plus button again. The simulator doesn't have a tiny water pump either — so we'll use an LED on pin 9 as a stand-in. When the LED lights up, picture the pump whirring and water flowing into the pot. Same code, same wiring — only the visible part is different from a real garden setup.
Click Start. Your program compiles and starts watching the soil.

Drag the sensor slider down low — that's our 'soil is dry' signal. The LED comes on, meaning the pump is running. Slide it back up high — 'soil is damp' — and the LED switches off, pump stops. The plant is now in charge of its own watering.

The reason the night lamp and the plant feel identical is that they are identical, deep down. Sensor reads a number. Program compares the number to a threshold. Output reacts. That same shape runs the thermostat in your house, the water-level alarm in a tank, the brightness sensor in a phone screen, the smoke detector on the ceiling. Once you've built it once in code, you've already built the bones of all of them. The work after that is just plugging in different sensors and different outputs.

Try this: change 400 to 200 — your plant only waters itself in really bone-dry soil. Or change to 700 — it waters at the slightest hint of dryness, like a fussy plant parent. Even better, swap the digitalWrite for an analogWrite and use two thresholds: very dry below 200 means a fast pump at full speed, dry below 400 means a gentle slow pump. One sensor, two reactions, even friendlier to the plant.

Two tutorials, same skeleton: analogRead pulls a number off a sensor, a threshold turns that number into a yes-or-no decision, digitalWrite makes an output react. You used it for light. You used it for moisture. The next time you meet a problem that smells like 'watch a sensor and react when it crosses a line', you already know the shape of the answer. That's pattern reuse — and it's the most powerful thing in programming.
